name: Putney, the smith's yard
certaintyNote: Cromwell was born in Putney, then a Thames-side village upstream of London, around 1485; the family's exact plot is not recorded, so this marks the riverside village near Brewhouse Lane where his father Walter kept a brewery and forge.
note: The story begins on the ground, with a beaten boy fleeing his violent father in a riverside village. Putney is where the future councillor starts as a blacksmith's son, and the distance from here to the King's chamber is the whole arc of the book.

sources.ref: Thomas Cromwell, Wikipedia, on his birth in Putney around 1485
Putney High Street, Wikipedia, for the village coordinates by the Thames

name: Cromwell's house, Austin Friars
certaintyNote: Cromwell built one of the City's largest private mansions within the Augustinian friary precinct at Austin Friars, but the house is gone; this marks the precinct centre at the surviving Dutch Church, raised in the old friary nave.
note: The book's warm heart is the busy household Cromwell runs in the City: clerks, wards, ledgers and a table always laid for one more guest. He enlarged his home out of the friary land at Austin Friars, and after his fall the Crown seized it. The precinct's church still stands as the Dutch Church.

sources.ref: Austin Friars, London, Wikipedia, on Cromwell's mansion within the friary precinct
Dutch Church, Austin Friars, Wikidata, for the precinct coordinates

name: York Place, becoming Whitehall
certaintyNote: York Place, the archbishops of York's London residence, passed to the King after Wolsey's fall and was rebuilt as the Palace of Whitehall; nothing Tudor survives above ground, so this marks the site at the Banqueting House, the one standing fragment.
note: When Cardinal Wolsey falls, the King takes his grand riverside house at York Place and makes it Whitehall, the new seat of the court. Cromwell moves with the power, from the cardinal's service into the King's, and the transfer of this one building marks the hinge of his rise.

sources.ref: Palace of Whitehall, Wikipedia, on York Place, Wolsey and the rebuilding as Whitehall
Banqueting House, Whitehall, latitude.to, for the site coordinates

name: The Tower of London
certaintyNote: The Tower is named in the novel and stands today, both fortress and state prison; sited at the White Tower at the centre of the complex.
note: The fortress downriver is where the King's business turns lethal. Thomas More is held and questioned here before his execution, and Cromwell is the man who must manage it. The Tower is the place the book keeps returning to when loyalty runs out.

sources.ref: White Tower, Tower of London, latitude.to, for the coordinates
Thomas Cromwell, Historic Royal Palaces, on Cromwell and the Tower

name: Hampton Court Palace
certaintyNote: Hampton Court is named and stands today; Wolsey built the palace and it passed to the King. It sits about twenty kilometres upriver, at the western edge of the book's Greater-London map.
note: Wolsey's country palace upriver is the measure of how high a cardinal could reach, and of how much the King wanted it. When it passes to Henry, the lesson is not lost on Cromwell: build magnificently, and be ready to hand it over. The Tudor palace still stands.

sources.ref: Hampton Court Palace, Wikipedia, on Wolsey's palace passing to the King
Hampton Court Palace, latitude.to, for the coordinates

name: Wolf Hall, Wiltshire
certaintyNote: Wulfhall, near Burbage in Wiltshire, was the Seymour family seat; the Tudor house is gone, and this marks its recorded site, roughly 110 kilometres west of London and well outside the book's London map.
note: The title names a place the reader barely reaches: the Seymour seat in Wiltshire, where the court will progress and where Jane Seymour waits at the edge of the story. It hangs over the last pages as the next move on the board, far from the City where Cromwell does his work.

sources.ref: Wulfhall, Wikipedia, on the Seymour family seat near Burbage
Wulfhall, latitude.to, for the site coordinates in Wiltshire
